title: Mariyayi Ammal vs Abdul Rahim Wakf Estate on 23 January, 2008
body: Mariyayi Ammal vs Abdul Rahim Wakf Estate on 23 January, 2008 Author: G.Rajasuria Bench: G.Rajasuria BEFORE THE MADURAI BENCH OF MADRAS HIGH COURT DATED: 23/01/2008 CORAM THE HONOURABLE MR.JUSTICE G.RAJASURIA S.A.No.1008 of 2000 and M.P.No.1 of 2008 1.Mariyayi Ammal 2.T.Balasubramanian 3.T.Balakrishnan 4.T.Natarajan ... Appellants/Appellants/ Defendants 4 to 7 Vs Abdul Rahim Wakf Estate, Trichy, rep. by its Present Administrator Mohammed Abdulla ... Respondent/Respondent/ Plaintiff Prayer Second Appeal filed under Section 100 of the Code of Civil Procedure, against the judgment and decree dated 24.04.2000 passed in A.S.No.129 of 1999 by the learned District Judge, Trichy, in confirming the judgment and decree dated 08.03.1999 passed in O.S.No.1427 of 1979 by the learned District Munsif, Tiruchirappalli. !For Appellants ... Mrs.N.Krishnaveni ^For Respondent ... Mrs.Hema Sampath :JUDGMENT This second appeal is focussed as against the judgment and decree dated 24.04.2000 passed in A.S.No.129 of 1999 by the learned District Judge, Trichy, in confirming the judgment and decree dated 08.03.1999 passed in O.S.No.1427 of 1979 by the learned District Munsif, Tiruchirappalli. 2. The parties, for convenience sake, are referred to hereunder according to their litigative status before the trial Court. 3. Broadly, but briefly, succinctly but narratively, the case of the plaintiff is that the pre-suit notice dated 24.01.1975 was issued by the plaintiff to the defendant calling upon him to vacate the suit property on the ground that the suit property was leased out to the defendant, but he unauthorisedly sub-leased it and also committed default in paying the rent. Since there was no positive response from the defendant, R.C.O.P.No.67 of 1977 was filed before the Rent Controller, Trichy, who by virtue of his order dated 19.04.1978, as found in Ex.B.1, the certified copy of it, dismissed the R.C.O.P, with the finding that only the plot was leased out to the defendant by the plaintiff and that only the landlord should file a civil suit before the competent Court. Whereupon, the plaintiff filed the suit in O.S.No.1427 of 1979 on the file of the District Munsif, Tiruchirappalli, seeking eviction setting out the aforesaid details, as against the deceased tenant Thangamani and his sub-tenants, D.2 and D.3. After the death of the said Thangamani, his legal representatives were added as D.4 to D.7. 4. The pith and marrow of the case of the contesting defendants would run thus: The suit is bad for want of a pre-suit statutory notice under Section 11 of the Tamil Nadu City Tenants' Protection Act. They also raised other factual pleas which are not germane for the disposal of this second appeal. 5. The trial Court framed as many as ten issues including the one relating to the maintainability of the suit for want of proper notice under Section 11 of the said Act. 6. During trial, P.W.1 was examined and Exs.A.1 and A.2 were marked on the side of the plaintiff and D.W.1 was examined and Ex.B.1 was marked on the side of the defendants. Exs.C.1 and C.2 were marked as Court documents. 7. Ultimately, the trial Court decreed the suit. 8. Being aggrieved by, the judgment and decree of the trial Court, the defendants 4 to 7 preferred the appeal before the District Court, Tiruchirappali, for nothing to be dismissed. 9. Being dissatisfied with, the judgments and decrees of both the Courts below, this second appeal is focussed on the grounds inter alia thus: Both the Courts below fell into error in not considering the significance of the non-issuance of statutory notice under Section 11 of the said Act. Simply because the tenant could not deposit the amount in time, there was no justification on the part of the Courts below in rejecting the tenant's right to purchase the land. Accordingly, they prayed for setting aside the judgments and decrees of both the Courts below and for dismissing the original suit. 10. At the time of admitting this second appeal, my learned Predecessor framed the following substantial questions of law: (i) Whether the Courts below were right in decreeing the suit for ejectment of the tenant when no notice under Section 11 of the Tami Nadu City Tenants Protection Act had been issued which is mandatory? (ii) Whether the Courts below were right in entertaining the suit when non-compliance of Section 11 is fatal to the same? 11. Heard both sides. 12. The unassailable and indubitable facts which took place during the pendency of the suit would run thus: (i) The legal heirs of the deceased Thangamani filed I.A.No.175 of 1982 under section 9 of the Act before the trial Court for the purpose of enabling them to purchase the land concerned over which the superstructure stands, on depositing the amount as might be fixed by the Advocate Commissioner. The Court by virtue of the order dated 19.04.1993, directed the plaintiff to sell the site to the tenant for Rs.7,78,500/- (Rupees Seven Lakhs Seventy Eight Thousand and Five Hundred only) and nine months time was given to the tenant to purchase it. (ii) However, the defendant did not deposit the amount. But, they filed I.A.No.669 of 1998 for extension of time to deposit the amount which was dismissed by the Court on 26.11.1998; whereupon C.R.P.No.1012 of 1999 was filed before this Court, but it was dismissed on 05.04.1999. Thereupon, I.A.No.474 of 1995 was filed by the plaintiff for appointment of a Commissioner to value the superstructure put up by the tenant so as to enable the plaintiff to purchase the superstructure. On 29.03.1996, the Commissioner filed his report, as against which objection was not filed by the tenant. Subsequently, I.A.No.1197 of 1996 was filed by the plaintiff seeking leave of the Court to deposit the value of the superstructure and accordingly, as per the Court order, the value was deposited by the plaintiff in the Court. 13. In this factual matrix, the learned Counsel for the appellants herein, would contend that de hors, the default committed by the appellants in depositing the amount for purchasing the site, they are entitled to get the original suit itself dismissed, on the ground that the suit was bad for want of a statutory notice under Section 11 of the said Act. 14. By way of fortifying and buttressing such a plea, the learned Counsel for the appellants cited the following decisions: ( i) Sri Agatheeswarar P.V. Perumal Devasthanam v. Narasimhan reported in 1982 (2) M.L.J 70. An excerpt from it, would run thus: "10. From the above discussion it is seen that section 11 of the Act is mandatory and a suit filed without the requisite notice under section 11 is liable to be dismissed but that the notice can be waived by the defendant either expressly or impliedly by his conduct and that the burden of proving that the defendant has either expressly or by implication waived the said notice is on the plaintiff. The question is whether the defendant in this case by his conduct in filing an application under section 9 can be taken to have waived the notice under section 11 so as to enable the plaintiff to maintain the suit notwithstanding the non-compliance with the provisions of section 11 . 11. In Vedachala Naicker v. Duraiswami Mudaliar [(1950) 1 M.L.J 732] it was held that the filing of an application by the tenant under section 9 of the Act amounts to a waiver by the tenant of the benefit conferred on him under section 11 . The said decision is based on the decision of the Privy Council in Vellayan Chettiar v. The Government of Madras [(1947) 2 M.L.J 208 : 74 I.A. 223 : 60 L.W. 630: A.I.R 1947 P.C 197.], which has already been referred to, laying down that the defect of non-compliance with the requirements of section 80 , Civil Procedure Code, were eschewed by reason of the waiver of the notice by the defendant for whose benefit the section was enacted. In Natesa Naicker v. Vedagiri [(1975) 1 M.L.J 301], this Court held that the filing of an application under section 9 by the tenant would itself amount to waiver of notice under section 11 . Therefore the question is whether the filing of an application under section 9 by the defendant in this case will amount to waiver of notice under section 11 . It is well-established that waiver is a question of fact and ought to be pleaded and proved in every case. No doubt the filing of an application under section 9 without any demur as regards the non-compliance with section 11 would amount to waiver of notice as the tenant's conduct in not raising the objection relating to notice. Taking advantage of the filing of the suit by the plaintiff the filing of an application under section 9 by the defendant would amount to a conduct from which the waiver of notice can be inferred. But in this the defendant has filed a written statement questioning the plaintiff's right to institute the suit and the jurisdiction of the Court to entertain the suit without a proper notice under section 11 . He has filed an application under section 9 by way of abundant as he was not sure whether his objection regarding the maintainability of the suit based on the non-compliance with Section 11 will be accepted by the Court or not. In the written statement, he has specifically stated that he is filing an application under Section 9 by way of abundant caution. He has not given up his benefit under Section 11 in the written statement either impliedly or expressly but specifically prays that the suit be dismissed for non-compliance with Section 11 . Therefore the filing of an application, in this case under Section 9 of the Act which is preceded by the filing of a written statement questioning the maintainability of the suit without a proper notice cannot amount to the waiver of the notice under Section 11. We have to therefore hold that in this case there has been no waiver of the benefit of section 11 by the defendant at any time. In this view the ultimate decision of the lower appellate Court that there is no waiver of the notice under Section 11 by the defendant is accepted as correct. The second appeal is therefore dismissed. There will, however, be no order as to costs." ( ii) Ramachandran, S.A v. S.Neelavathy reported in 1997(I) CTC 298. An excerpt from it, would run thus: "14. We are of the view that since the requirements under Section 11 are in the nature of conditions precedent which had to be complied with before instituting a suit in a court, the non-compliance would be fatal and such suit would be liable to be dismissed at the threshold. The requirements under Section 11 cannot be compared with the requirements under Section 80 C.P.C. The right of a tenant who may, if not always, be a poor tenant, cannot be compared with the mighty Governments regarding whom it has been observed times out of number by the Courts that they should not litigate with their citizens on technicalities and should not endeavour to defeat the suit by pleading the technical questions, as for example, want of notice under Section 80 C.P.C or limitation in cases which are eminently just, proper and equitable. The Act essentially is for the protection of the tenants whereas no such protection for the Government is contemplated by Section 80 of the Code of Civil Procedure. But without entering into this controversy any further and leaving the question of waiver open, we may, in this case, observe that on facts there is no waiver." 15. Placing reliance on the aforesaid precedents, the learned Counsel for the appellants would develop her arguments that mere filing of the application under Section 9 of the Act by the legal representatives of the tenant, would not act as an estoppel in pressing their plea that the suit should be dismissed for want of statutory notice under section 11 of the Act and that such steps taken by the defendants in filing the application under Section 9 of the Act, on the apprehension that the original suit might be decreed as against them, cannot be viewed as a waiver of their statutory right. 16. Per contra, denying and refuting the contentions on the side of the appellants and by way of torpedoing the arguments put forth on the side of the appellants, the learned Senior Counsel for the respondent/landlord would submit that the Honourable Apex Court in the decision referred to supra, left open the plea of waiver to be considered by the Courts depending upon the factual matrix of a case; the tenant and his legal representatives waived their right to insist for the issuance of statutory notice under Section 11 of the Act in view of they having filed the application under Section 9 of the Act; their failure in depositing the amount assessed relating to the site to be purchased by the tenant; their filing of the extension petition which was dismissed and their filing of the Civil Revision Petition which was also dismissed by the High Court. 17. The learned Senior Counsel for the respondent would cite the following decisions: ( i) Hamsa Patel v. S.Balakrishnan reported in 1997 (I) CTC 367. An excerpt from it, would run thus: "14. ... We are therefore, clearly of the opinion, that defendants 1 and 2 as well as the fifth defendant have miserably failed to prove that either of them were in possession of the property entitling them to file an application under Section 9 of the Act. Once it is held that defendants/appellants are not entitled to purchase the property under Section 9 of the Act, the decree for possession has to be upheld. A plea, in frustration, was sought to be taken that no notice under Section 11 of the Act was issued to the fifth defendant. Such a plea was never taken in the Courts below and we are of the opinion that the fifth defendant cannot be permitted to raise only such plea at this stage. That a part having filed an application under Section 9 of the Act and the application having been considered on merits, she is stopped from raising the issue of notice under Section 11 of the Act. This proposition is also laid down by the decision of this Court in Natesa Naicker v. Vedagiri , 1975 (I) MLJ 301, P.R.Gokulakrishnan, J. observes: "If they are not tenants under the respondent herein, there is no necessity to give notice under section 106 of the Transfer of Property Act, as contended by Thiru.N.C.Raghavachari. As regards notice under Section 11 of the Act, it is clear from the decision in Vedachala Naicker v. Duraiswami Mudaliar , 1950 (I) MLJ 732, that the tenants have lost their right by waiver of such notice, inasmuch as they had filed applications under Section 9 of the Act." (ii) Vedachala Naicker v. Duraiswamy Mudaliar reported in 1950 (1) MLJ 732. An excerpt from it, would run thus: "Further it must be borne in mind that the provisions of Section 11 of the Act were enacted for the benefit of the tenant as pointed out in the rulings referred to above and not for his detriment and the landlord therefore cannot take advantage of this provision to the prejudice of the tenant. It is open to the tenant to waive the protection afforded to him under that section and request the court to grant him the relief provided for in section 9 of the Act. In Venkataswami v. Mahalakshmi [(1949) 1 MLJ 601] it was laid down by Viswanatha Sastriar, J. that waiver might be express or implied from conduct and that a person who had taken a plea based on lack of notice under Section 80 , could waive it and allow the suit to proceed as if the requirements of Section 80 have been complied with. It is also stated there by the learned Judge that this can be done so long as a suit is not finally disposed of, rejecting the contention that there could be no waiver after objection as to want of notice had been raised in the written statement. I am therefore of opinion that in this case the filing of an application under Section 9 of the Act amounts to a waiver by the tenant of the benefit conferred on him under Section 11 of the Act." 18. The learned Senior Counsel for the respondent would also submit that this is a peculiar case in which the facts are different from the facts involved in the decision cited supra on the side of the appellants herein, as in this case, the facts are that the legal representatives of the tenant tried their level best to get the extension of time to deposit the amount even by approaching this Court by filing the Civil Revision Petition which was dismissed. 19. A thorough reading of the decision of the Honourable Apex Court as well as the decision of the Division Bench of this Court cited on the side of the appellants, would at once make the point clear that the facts in this case are peculiar in themselves as in those cited cases, the tenant did not commit any default in paying the amount assessed for purchasing the site. But, in this case, peculiarly the legal representatives of the tenant committed gross default in not complying with the order of the Court. However, they prayed for extension of time which was dismissed by the Court and as against which, the Civil Revision Petition was filed which was also dismissed by the High Court on 05.04.1999. An excerpt from the said order in C.R.P.No.1012 of 1998, is reproduced hereunder for ready reference: "... No power has been confirmed under Section 9(ii)(b) to extend the period beyond the maximum period of three years prescribed in the said section. It is not as if a right has not accrued but a right has accrued to the original defendant on 19.04.1993, after which date the original defendant died and his legal representatives were brought on record. It is fairly admitted that the revision petitioners have not taken steps to extend the time to deposit till 29.06.1998, which is definitely after a period of four years. The statutory provision enables the Court to extend the time if any for a period of three years. Hence, the Court below had rightly dismissed the application. I do not find any illegality or material irregularity in the order passed by the Court. 2. The counsel for the Caveator represents that the market value of the superstructure has been fixed by the Court below and the respondent landlord had already deposited the market value of the superstructure in terms of the provisions of the Act. 3. No case has been made out for interference under Section 115 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. Hence this revision petition fails. Consequently, C.M.P.No.5536 of 1999 is also dismissed." 20. The above excerpt would unambiguously highlight that the defendant by their own conduct to wit, latches and default lost their right to purchase the site. After meeting with their waterloo in their attempt to purchase the site, they cannot veer round and take a retrograde step and fall back on their erstwhile plea of non-issuance of statutory notice under Section 11 of the Act. The statutory time limit also was considered by the High Court and held that it got expired and consequently dismissed the said Civil Revision Petition. 21. It is a trite proposition of law that a litigant after adhering to a course of action and failed to get relief, cannot try to get the same relief by trying to invoke the plea based on a statutory plea which they waived by their own action earlier. If the plea of the appellants herein are accepted, it would amount to giving one more opportunity to the appellants to purchase the site which right they got it lost, even though the Court already granted such relief. 22. The High Court has already pointed out that the appellants' right got barred by limitation, because of their own inaction and in such a case, this is a peculiar case by itself which would not attract the precedents cited on the side of the appellants and obviously the defendants cannot take protection under such precedents. 23. Nowhere in any of those decisions cited on the side of the plaintiff, it was held that despite the tenant having lost his right by his own latches in getting the site purchased from the landlord, he could ask for one more opportunity by calling upon the plaintiff to issue notice under Section 11 of the Act so as to enable the tenant to exercise the right of purchase. 24. At this juncture, I would recollect the following maxim: (i) Cuilibet licet renuntiare juri pro so introducto. (A person could forego voluntarily the benefit conferred on him without infringing any public right or public policy.) 25. The aforesaid maxim would highlight that the right could be waived or renounced by an individual, provided it is not against public policy. Here, the aforesaid decisions of the Honourable Apex Court and this Court, would also unambiguously highlight and spotlight the fact that the concept of waiver is very much available relating to the relinquishment of the statutory notice contemplated under Section 11 of the Act, by the tenant concerned. 26. Here, to the risk of repetition without being tautologous, I would reiterate that the defendant after choosing a specific course of action and pursuing it to its logical end and ultimately, meeting with its failure on the ground of limitation and his own latches, cannot seek for one more opportunity by trying to animate a dead plea based on Section 11 of the said Act. 27. Both the Courts below properly adverted to these aspects and gave their findings that the defendant lost that opportunity to purchase the site and in such a case, no interference is required. 28. The learned Counsel for the appellants would try to find fault with the judgments and decrees of both the Courts below on the main ground that there was no discussion much less sufficient discussion relating to the issue based on Section 11 of the Act. In paragraph No.11 of the judgment of the trial Court, the trial Court adverted to the plea of the parties and in its own summary way, decided the issue which the appellate Court confirmed. A mere lack of elaborate discussion would not render the judgments and decrees of both the Courts below illegal or perverse and that too when the law points and facts as discussed supra, are clear to the effect that there was waiver on the part of the defendants. 29. During the pendency of this second appeal, M.P.No.1 of 2008 was filed by the third parties under Order 1, Rule 10(1) C.P.C , so as to get themselves included as respondents, on the main ground that the suit property belongs to Wakf and that there was previous scheme suit between the plaintiff in this case and the proposed petitioners in M.P.No.1 of 2008 an that they should also be added as respondents in this case. 30. It is ex facie and prima facie, clear from the above discussion as to how the plaintiff herein has all along been making sincere efforts in all the three Courts in defending the rights over the suit property and there is no collusion between the plaintiff and the defendants herein and in such a case, there is no rhyme or reason on the petitioners herein to get themselves impleaded in the second appeal and hence, M.P.No.1 of 2008 is liable to be dismissed and accordingly dismissed. 31. In the result, this second appeal is dismissed. However, there is no order as to costs. rsb To 1.The District Judge, Trichy. 2.The District Munsif, Tiruchirappalli. 
